  "trigger": "When the question is a 'when' question requesting a date/year and at least one summary contains an explicit phrase tying the subject to becoming a rap rock band (e.g. summary_2 states: \"Limp Bizkit became a rap rock band ...\").",
  "preserve_rule": "Keep all existing formatting, the two-block output contract, the requirement to number reasoning items, and the exact citation phrasings for summary_1 and summary_2. Continue to include the single required question-type metadata item in the reasoning block.",
  "change_rule": "If any summary explicitly uses a 'became' phrasing that supplies a year for when the subject 'became' the requested category, prefer that explicit 'became' year as the basis for the [[ ## answer ## ]] token. When summaries conflict on the year, choose the year that is explicitly presented as the time the subject 'became' the category rather than inferring it from a formation date or other event. Reflect that choice in the reasoning by citing the summary that supplies the explicit 'became' phrasing.",
  "drop_or_avoid_rule": "Do not infer that the formation year equals the year the subject 'became' the category unless a summary explicitly states they are the same. Avoid favoring formation dates or other related events over an explicit 'became' statement when one is present.",
  "output_contract": "Do not change the required two-block output format or the minimal-answer constraint. The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must still contain only the single minimal year token derived from the reasoning and nothing else.",
  "why_local_transition": "This narrows the decision rule for date queries to prefer explicit 'became' statements in the summaries. That small behavioral bias makes the agent select a year given as the time the band 'became' a rap rock band (when such an explicit statement appears) instead of defaulting to a formation date."
}

EDGE 1: 1997 -> 1999
{
  "trigger": "Apply when the question asks \"when\" (contains 'When did' or 'when did') and at least one summary mentions either formation or an explicit becoming statement about the same entity (e.g., contains the phrase 'became a rap rock band' or 'formed').",
  "preserve_rule": "Preserve the base prompt's exact output format, the numbered-reasoning structure, the required attribution phrasing (\"summary_1 states:\" / \"summary_2 states:\"), and the final [[ ## answer ## ]] block contract.",
  "change_rule": "When forming premises, require an explicit summary statement that links the status-change verb ('became', 'became a', 'became an') to a year in order to infer the date of 'becoming' (e.g., use a summary that states 'became a rap rock band in <year>' as the authoritative basis). If a summary explicitly states such a link, extract that year verbatim (canonicalize to the same numeric token shown in the summaries). If no summary explicitly links 'became' (or an equivalent phrase) to a year and the only date present is a formation date (e.g., 'formed in <year>'), do not infer that formation==became; instead conclude 'insufficient information'. Add one short numbered premise that documents this decision when applicable (and attribute it to the summary that provides or lacks the explicit link).",
  "drop_or_avoid_rule": "Avoid inferring a 'became' date from a formation date unless a summary explicitly ties them together. Do not substitute or guess years that are not explicitly stated as the 'became' year in the provided summaries.",
  "output_contract": "Keep the two-block output exactly. If an explicit 'became' year is supported by the summaries, the [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that year token (as shown in the summaries). If no explicit 'became' year is present, the [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain exactly: insufficient information. Do not add punctuation or extra words.",
  "why_local_transition": "This change removes a single implicit inference (treating formation date as the becoming date) and instead requires explicit textual linkage in the summaries. It is a minimal behavioral change focused on how the agent maps summary statements to the requested 'became' date, preventing incorrect inferences while preserving all other reasoning and formatting rules."
}

Metrics are evidence of functional consequence,\nnot the distance definition itself.\n\nA midpoint output o_mid between o_left and o_right must:\n- preserve useful task-relevant behavior or information from o_left;\n- make concrete semantic progress toward o_right;\n- differ meaningfully from both endpoint outputs;\n- not merely paraphrase or copy o_left;\n- not simply copy o_right;\n- represent two smaller functional transitions than the original edge.\n\nGenerate one midpoint output between the left and right projected states.\nThe midpoint output will be used directly as the destination output for a\nlocal prompt-update inference step.     "rendered_prompt": "You are working with a functional semantic distance over states of one\nagent in a multi-agent pipeline.\n\nEach state contains the selected agent's output and may also contain the\ndownstream consequences produced by that output while the rest of the\npipeline is held fixed. The agent output is the primary object of the\ntransition. Downstream evidence and metrics are supporting evidence about\nits functional effect.\n\nThe distance is not surface text edit distance and is not a correctness\nscore. It measures how much task-relevant behavior and information must\nchange to move from one state to another.\n\nJudge distance using:\n1. Agent-output behavior: what the output is trying to accomplish for the\n   selected agent's role.\n2. Task-relevant information: which entities, relations, evidence,\n   conclusions, or answer constraints are preserved, added, removed, or\n   corrected.\n3. Downstream consequence: how the output changes retrieval, evidence,\n   answerability, or information available to later agents.\n4. Transition utility: whether a state is a meaningful intermediate step\n   between two endpoint states.\n\nDo not infer distance from text length, token count, document count, or\nlexical overlap. The agent output is the primary object of the\ntransition. Downstream evidence and metrics are supporting evidence about\nits functional effect.\n\nThe distance is not surface text edit distance and is not a correctness\nscore. It measures how much task-relevant behavior and information must\nchange to move from one state to another.\n\nJudge distance using:\n1. Agent-output behavior: what the output is trying to accomplish for the\n   selected agent's role.\n2. Task-relevant information: which entities, relations, evidence,\n   conclusions, or answer constraints are preserved, added, removed, or\n   corrected.\n3. Downstream consequence: how the output changes retrieval, evidence,\n   answerability, or information available to later agents.\n4. Transition utility: whether a state is a meaningful intermediate step\n   between two endpoint states.\n\nDo not infer distance from text length, token count, document count, or\nlexical overlap.   "change_rule": "When the trigger matches and the question asks for a birthplace/place name, prefer the more specific supported form by appending the explicit administrative subdivision present in the same summary to the place name in both the reasoning normalization and the final answer.   "drop_or_avoid_rule": "Do not invent, infer, or add any administrative unit that is not explicitly present in the summaries.
